scoring_notes.SCI: State Commitment Index. Federal budget activity + state-linked contractor starts + 2% mortgage volume as state expenditure.
scoring_notes.track_a: Private market signal. Non-state developer entry + cash DKP transactions (non-mortgage) + asking price momentum. Mariupol penalised: Bellingcat/Shumanov confirm near-zero genuine private entry.
scoring_notes.track_b: Residential outcome signal. Secondary listing depth (Domclick counts) + buyer permanence score (PSB/VTB disclosures) + rental-to-purchase ratio (estimated where marked).
scoring_notes.composite: SCI × 0.4 + Track A × 0.6. Weighted blend of state commitment and private market signal.
scoring_notes.spread: Track B minus Composite (SCI×0.4 + A×0.6). Positive = residential confidence LEADS state+private composite. Negative = composite OUTPACES residential reality — the fragility signal.
scoring_notes.data_quality: real = sourced and dateable. estimated = calibrated from anchors. uncertain = low-confidence estimate.

crimea.notes: Moscow red line. 1.2x weight. Decade-integrated market. Price data fully real from Restate.ru.

history.note: Jun 21 AM: Track A 54→50, composite 57→55, spread −11→−9. Hotel booking collapse confirmed worsening (31% overall, 40% Sevastopol). | Jun 21 PM: Track B 46→38, spread −9→−17. First substantive Track B movement of the monitoring period. Total civilian fuel access suspension (Aksyonov/Razvozhayev, 9am Jun 21) directly undermines the residential-permanence premise — 30-year mortgage commitment hard to rationalize where legal civilian fuel purchase is currently impossible. Composite stays 55 (SCI and Track A unchanged). RPI held at 96 — fuel ban is distribution/access policy downstream of existing RPI pressure, not new refinery/export infrastructure damage. | Jun 24: Track A 50→47, Track B 38→35, composite 55→53, spread −17→−18. Sevastopol full city blackout after main substation destroyed overnight Jun 23–24. North Crimean Canal railway bridge destroyed (SSO two-phase strike). Black Sea Fleet families evacuating to Novorossiysk. Tourism/summer camp season effectively cancelled. Multi-modal civilian infrastructure failure: fuel + power + transport simultaneously. | Jul 3: Track B 35→33, spread −18→−20. Geographic spread of infrastructure failure confirmed beyond Sevastopol: Armyansk without power and water 2+ days, Feodosia municipal district significant outages, Sevastopol power restored then re-cut within 24 hours. Peninsula-wide failure, not city-specific. 3,500-vehicle queue confirmed at Kerch Bridge (Inkorr Jul 2). Residential permanence premise further eroded by sustained multi-city collapse. | Jul 9: Track A 47→44. Kyiv Post/Korshak reports Crimea real estate prices down 30–50% since January 2026 during peak summer season — confirmed demand collapse. Tourism reservations down 79% in June; rail and ferry suspended. Private market confidence in active collapse, not mere stagnation. | Jul 9: Track B →30, spread →−21. Peninsula-wide Crimea blackout Jul 6 (Krymenergo confirmed regionwide). Sevastopol kindergartens cut from 74 to 24 operating — first-order civic services failure. Fuel rationing confirmed in all four occupied oblasts. Multi-commodity food shortages confirmed (sugar, flour, cereals, salt, pasta). Grey market fuel via Telegram/Avito. Aksyonov Jul 8: crisis 'open-ended,' fuel unavailability on certain days permanent. Residents describing 'survival mode' (Le Monde Jul 9). 30–50% real estate price collapse corroborates residential permanence rejection. | Jul 9: MTCS at 80 (acute boundary — threshold is >80; band remains Fragility Zone). Base 78 + OFP +2 = 80. ECS at ceiling (100). Crimea in active evacuation. Behavioral change underway, not anticipated — state of emergency, civilian exodus, industrial shutdowns, civic services failure all confirmed. | Jul 17: Track A 44→42. Don-Azov Canal suspended Jul 10–11; Sea of Azov vessel count fell from 132 to 43 (55% AIS transponder decline); maritime blockade of Crimea supply routes now complete — land corridors and sea routes simultaneously closed. No new private market transaction data but macro environment for any private activity has materially worsened beyond Jul 9 baseline. | Jul 17: Track B 30→28. Confirmed persistence and deepening of civilian infrastructure failure: water supply limited to one hour daily in some areas (Jul 11); farms halting mechanized operations due to combined electricity and fuel failures (Chubarov/ISW Jul 14); rotating 2-on/6-off blackouts in Sevastopol (Razvozhayev Jul 14); transformer cannibalization — ATESH Jul 14 reports Russian authorities removing transformers from idle Northern Crimean Canal pumping stations to replace destroyed substations, spare stocks exhausted; VTB ATM withdrawal limits reduced for Crimea customers. These are not disruptions — they are the new baseline. Residential permanence premise structurally impaired beyond prior floor. | Jul 17: MTCS crosses acute threshold (>80). Base 78 + OFP +4 = 82. OFP modulator advances from +2 to +4 as fiscal pressure crosses into meaningful-strain band. ECS at ceiling (100). Crimea in active evacuation — maritime blockade complete, water/power/fuel simultaneously failed, agricultural and emergency services affected. Behavioral change confirmed and deepening, not anticipated.

berdiansk.notes: Thin data. All estimates. Port opened to international shipping Aug 2025.

key_events.label: 2% secondary market window closes (Apr 30 deadline passed)
VTB enters occupied territories
Berdiansk and Mariupol ports opened to international ships (Russia decree)
Rosreestr: 550,000 properties registered as ownerless in occupied Ukraine
Minfinance decree: 2% program extended to end-2030
Central bank rate cut to 15.5% — sixth consecutive cut
Kommersant: occupied territory mortgage volumes now comparable to Crimea
ЕИСЖС: DNR now 25 developers, 68 buildings active (vs 10/16 one year prior)
Crimea Restate index: −5.6% single period
Crimea Restate index: +11.2% (peak 251,273 ₽/m²)
Crimea Restate index: −12.3% — largest single-period drop in 12 months
Crimea Restate index: 221,332 ₽/m² — current reading
Occupied Luhansk imposes 20L/person fuel rationing (gasoline + diesel) — mirrors Crimea restrictions, cites logistics bottlenecks
Russian fuel rationing spreads to 20+ regions including Moscow and St. Petersburg — ~40% primary refining capacity offline; Crimea crisis is leading edge of national crisis
Ukraine strikes cargo vessels in Mariupol/Berdiansk ports — 5 Azerbaijani crew members killed (BBC/GlobalSecurity)
Mariupol port disabled by Ukrainian strikes — substations, radar, fuel storage, control tower hit (Azov Corps)
Sevastopol fuel rationing cut to 20L/week (from 20L/day) — QR-code system via Max messenger app, worst fuel crisis since 2014
Ukrainian drones strike Sevastopol Panorama Museum alongside Mariupol port and Russian oil refineries — governor Razvozhayev confirms damage
R-280 Crimea corridor traffic down 71% in two weeks per Ukrainian drone command — primary GLOC to Crimea via Mariupol/Berdiansk
Sevastopol fuel rationing coupons fully suspended — tanker trucks unable to reach city after corridor strikes; refueling restricted to emergency and essential services only

Crimea food/fuel crisis confirmed by Ukrainian govt official: гречка/rice/salt/sugar shortages, ~30% dairy self-sufficiency, few weeks of food stock remaining, real estate agency offering villa stays for fuel canisters

Ukraine strikes Moscow Kapotnya oil refinery — second hit in one week, four airports halted operations; fuel rationing now confirmed in 53 Russian regions; crude processing at 21-year low per Energy Intelligence

555-drone attack: Kapotnya refinery struck second time in one week, Gukovo oil depot hit, four Moscow airports halted, five refinery locations damaged — ISW confirms fires at combined oil refining unit and oil tank farm

Russia begins importing gasoline from Asia by sea — first time; previously managed domestic supply without imports in 2025, confirming 2026 shortage is materially worse. ~33% of refining capacity offline before this attack.
Putin signs FEZ investment threshold reduction 30%→10% across occupied Donetsk, Luhansk, Zaporizhzhia, Kherson — explicit legislative acknowledgment that private investment attraction has failed
North Crimean Canal railway bridge destroyed — SSO two-phase strike (initial strike + follow-up destroyed repair equipment); SSO: 'bridge no longer exists'
Sevastopol full city blackout — main substation (330/220/110/35 kV) destroyed overnight Jun 23–24; public transport curfew 10pm, shops close 8pm, summer camps suspended to Sep 1
Black Sea Fleet families evacuating Crimea to Novorossiysk — traffic jams in both directions on Kerch Bridge; bridge closed 5+ hours after Kerch port oil depot fires
Russian fuel crisis reaches Siberia — Omsk caps purchases at 40L, Irkutsk in 'manual mode' rationing, Novosibirsk, Kamchatka, Yakutia join ban list; at least 15 regions now rationing
Moscow Exchange −5%, ruble past 75/dollar (weakest since May 6), Gazprom shares below 100 rubles (lowest since 2009), OFZ bond prices fall — market reaction to deepening fuel/energy crisis
Orenburg Gazprom gas processing plant struck overnight Jun 23–24 — accounts for 60% of Gazprom Pererabotka gas processing capacity; Russia's only helium plant hit; 1,200km from front
R-280 Novorossiya highway closed km 317–331 (occupied Zaporizhzhia) after Ukrainian drone strikes — primary land supply corridor to Crimea via Mariupol/Berdiansk; traffic diverted through villages
Three bridges struck overnight Jun 24–25: road bridge over Korsak River (Zaporizhzhia), railway bridges over Aidar and Luhanchyk rivers (Luhansk Oblast)
Ukraine launches SBU-authorized 40-day intermediate and long-range strike operation — Jun 26 strikes hit two cable ships, ferry near Kerch, multiple Crimea substations, Kerch airfield (ISW Jun 26)
NORSI refinery halted — Russia's 4th largest (Kstovo, Nizhny Novgorod), CDU-5 primary refining unit damaged, 15M ton/year capacity offline (Reuters/Ukrainska Pravda Jun 25)
Ufa dual-refinery strikes: Bashneft-Novoil and Bashneft-Ufaneftekhim — combined 10.5M tons annual processing capacity (OSINT/ISW Jun 26)
Fuel rationing confirmed in at least 56 Russian regions (up from 53); Moscow Oil Refinery offline minimum 6 months after Jun 16/18 attacks (EA WorldView Jun 25)

Russia negotiating emergency gasoline imports from Kazakhstan (~50,000 MT) and drafting tax subsidies for India imports — unprecedented; domestic production gap cannot be closed by existing measures (Reuters/Counteroffensive Jun 25)
Baltic/Black Sea crude loadings on track for record ~2.7M bpd in June (up from 2.5M May) — damaged refineries redirecting unprocessed crude to export markets (Energy News Pro/Inkorr Jun 25)
Public transit suspended in occupied Crimea and four Russian oblasts (Zabaikalsky, Kaluga, Rostov, Tula) due to diesel shortages (NV Ukraine/Agentstvo Jun 25)
Satellite imagery confirms kilometer-long vehicle queues exiting Crimea via Kerch Bridge (Kyiv Independent Jun 25)

Aksyonov and Razvozhayev sign formal state of emergency decrees in occupied Crimea and Sevastopol — primarily to streamline supply procurement and bypass bureaucratic restrictions on energy restoration (ISW Jun 26)
TES fuel network employees formally petition Putin for company rescue from collapse — signals fuel retail infrastructure financially failing, not merely constrained (Inkorr Jun 26)

PSB: more branches per capita in occupied Ukraine than Moscow Oblast; 2,000 small business loans approved in occupied Zaporizhzhia H1 2026, up 50% YoY — state financial infrastructure deepening as physical infrastructure collapses (ISW Jun 25)

Ukraine FIS reports internal Russian documents showing near-daily governance failures in occupied Crimea; over 80% of Russians reportedly consider large-scale economic crisis inevitable [unverified — Ukrainian intelligence source] (ISW Jun 26)

WATCH: reports of Russian military forces transitioning from formal tenancy/subsidized purchase toward commandeering civilian property for billeting in some occupied areas — pattern across multiple digest inputs, no single named-source confirmation (monitor watch condition)

Putin publicly admits Crimea has ‘only days’ of fuel reserves and Russia is ‘going through a difficult period’ — first presidential-level confirmation of acute Crimea supply crisis; pledges increased land and sea deliveries (Guardian Jun 29, Reuters Jun 29)

Slavyansk-na-Kubani refinery struck (Krasnodar Krai) — ~4M ton/year capacity, named key Crimea fuel supplier; fire ongoing Jun 29; Yaroslavl refinery struck same night (ABC News/AP Jun 28, Kyiv Independent Jun 29)

ISW confirms Mariupol as epicenter of Russia’s resettlement campaign — Russian citizens relocated into city as Russification strategy; coercive passportization pressure confirmed across all occupied oblasts (ISW Jun 29, Just Security Jun 29)

Donetsk–Mariupol highway bridge near Kremenivka/Hranitne destroyed — multiple spans collapsed, route fully blocked, Russian logistics rerouted +15–20km; second bridge near Novoazovsk on R-280 also partially collapsed same day (Moscow Times/Militarnyi Jul 1)

Ukrainian MoD discloses June 2026 DeepStrike campaign: 11 refineries, 7 fuel logistics facilities, 8 defense-industrial enterprises struck; maximum confirmed range 2,000+km (Ukrainian MoD via United24/Ukrinform Jul 1)
Russia crude oil processing fell 25% YoY to 3.95M bpd in June — lowest level in over two decades (AP/Economic Times Jul 1)

Bloomberg: Russian seaborne crude exports hit record 4.13M bpd in June while gross export revenues fell to $1.9B/week (lowest since March 2026) — volume cannot compensate for price collapse; refineries redirecting unprocessed crude to export (ISW Jul 1)
Russia begins importing refined gasoline from India for first time — 60,000–80,000 MT en route, plans for 400,000 MT/month from multiple countries (Reuters/ISW Jul 1)

Armyansk without power and water 2+ days; Feodosia municipal district significant outages; Sevastopol power restored then re-cut within 24 hours — infrastructure failure now peninsula-wide, not city-specific (ISW Jul 1)
Occupied Luhansk social fund pays 880,000 rubles to six Far North residents for relocation to occupied territory — monetary incentives for Russian civilian resettlement deepening (ISW Jul 2)
‘Zemsky Teacher’ program recruits Russian teachers to occupied Kherson Oblast at 2M rubles each — Russification through institutional staffing (ISW Jul 2)

USF 48-hour operation Jul 1–2 disables 13 energy targets: 10 electrical substations in occupied Crimea, one each in occupied Luhansk and Donetsk oblasts, gas distribution station near Aidar (USF Commander Brovdi/Kyiv Independent Jul 2)

NORSI refinery (Kstovo, Nizhny Novgorod) struck second time in 8 days — AVT-6 primary crude unit on fire; CDU-5 unit was knocked offline in Jun 24 strike; facility now multiply degraded (Euromaidan Press/ISW Jul 2)

Sevastopol actual pump price reaches 199 rubles/liter (Kommersant Jul 2) — nearly double official Rosstat figure of 118.79 rubles/liter; Razvozhayev confirms rationing continues minimum one more month
Bilohirsk quarry (occupied Crimea) halts operations entirely due to fuel absence — first confirmed industrial production shutdown attributable to fuel crisis (RFE/RL/ISW Jul 2)
Novorossiysk — Russia's primary Black Sea oil terminal (~980,000 bpd crude loadings) — zero gasoline at all public filling stations; shortage began June 10 (Meduza Jul 3)
Sevastopol kindergartens cut from 74 to 24 operating due to fuel and electricity constraints — first-order civic services failure (Euromaidan Press Jul 3)
Multi-commodity food shortages confirmed across Crimea: sugar, flour, cereals, salt, pasta rationed; food prices roughly doubled at some stores (Euromaidan Press Jul 3)

Putin approval rating falls 3.5 pts to 66.9% week of Jun 22–28 — steepest single-week decline since Feb 2022; 55% of Russians report anxiety in those around them, highest since Sep 2022 mobilization (ISW Jul 3, VTsIOM/Levada)
St. Petersburg oil terminal struck by Ukrainian drone Jul 4; Kerch ferry terminal also struck (ISW Jul 5)

Kerch and Feodosia occupation administrations ordered to evacuate valuable documents and equipment by Jul 4; some officials departing for Krasnodar Krai [unverified — Ukrainian partisan source: Atesh/ISW Jul 3]

Peninsula-wide Crimea blackout Jul 6 — largest single-night drone attack of war (47 targets struck); Krymenergo confirmed regionwide power failure; Simferopol 330kV substation, two S-400 systems in Kerch, two shadow fleet tankers struck (ISW/USF Brovdi Jul 6)

Omsk Gazprom Neft refinery struck — Russia's largest, 12% of national refining output, 2,500km from border; ELOU-AVT-11 primary refining unit on fire; deepest strike of war. Ukraine has now struck all 11 largest Russian gasoline producers (ISW/Reuters Jul 6)
ISW confirms 42.7% of total Russian projected refining capacity now disabled as of early July (ISW Jul 6)

Slavneft-Yanos Yaroslavl refinery (15M ton/year) and Novatek Ust-Luga Baltic complex struck Jul 6; Vysotsk seaport damaged, two oil loading arms disabled, three product reservoirs struck (NV Ukraine/ISW Jul 6)
Fuel rationing confirmed in all four occupied Ukrainian oblasts (Luhansk, Donetsk, Zaporizhzhia, Kherson) as of Jul 6 (ISW Jul 6)

19 Russian fuel tankers struck in Sea of Azov over 72 hours as Russia pivoted Crimea resupply from damaged M-14 highway to seaborne routes from Mariupol/Berdiansk [unverified — Ukrainian partisan source]; SBU Sea Baby drone struck sanctioned Suezmax tanker Blue near Yalta Jul 8 (SBU/Mezha Jul 9)

Russia imposes diesel export ban effective Jul 8–31 — Deputy PM Novak confirmed at cabinet meeting with Putin; national policy response confirming domestic production tools exhausted (AFP/Reuters Jul 8)

Aksyonov publicly confirms Crimea fuel crisis is open-ended: 'on certain days there will be no fuel available to be freely sold' — first explicit indefinite admission by occupation governor (Reuters Jul 8)
Crimea real estate prices down 30–50% since January 2026 during peak summer season (Kyiv Post/Korshak Jul 8) — first direct price data confirming demand collapse

17 Chinese companies documented embedded in occupied Donetsk, Luhansk, Zaporizhzhia, Kherson through Russian intermediaries — mining equipment, Huawei telecom infrastructure, yuan-denominated banking at ~79 branches; 'shadow integration' deepening occupied territory economy (Washington Times Jul 8)
Over 90% of Russian regions have experienced fuel rationing or shortages since June; Bank of Russia flags fuel prices as driver of inflation above target (Vanguard/AFP Jul 8)
Crimea receives only one-third of daily fuel needs — Razvozhayev confirms; fuel prices rose 78.4% in single week per Russian Statistics Service (Mezha.net/Kyiv Independent Jul 9)

36 Russian shadow fleet vessels struck in Sea of Azov Jul 6–9, targeting tankers on Taganrog-to-Crimea fuel route — ISW: ‘new phase’ of Crimea isolation campaign combining land and maritime interdiction (BBC/ISW Jul 9)

Luhansk Oblast occupation officials publish Jun 16 decree simplifying ‘ownerless’ property designation — enabling broader seizure and redistribution; Rosreestr identifies 1.9M properties missing from state register and 63,000 unused land parcels across occupied oblasts (ISW Jul 9)

618 residential properties transferred to law enforcement, healthcare, and education personnel in occupied Zaporizhzhia Oblast; first Mariupol apartment under municipal co-financing program transferred Jul 3 (ISW Jul 9)

Ilsky refinery (Krasnodar Krai, ~3M ton/year, primary Southern Federal District supplier via Novorossiysk/Taman) struck overnight Jul 9–10; Moynaki substation Yevpatoria struck, cutting city power (Censor.NET Jul 10)
Don-Azov Canal navigation halted following vessel strikes — effectively closing the maritime resupply route Russia pivoted to after R-280 highway and bridge degradation (Reuters Jul 10–11)

76 Russian vessels struck in Sea of Azov Jul 6–11; 21 tankers in single overnight operation Jul 10–11; AIS data shows 55% decline in active vessel transponders over period (ISW Jul 11, Starboard Maritime Intelligence)

Water supply limited to one hour daily in some Crimea areas; grey-market gasoline $25/gallon via Telegram/Avito/Ozon; shops closing or cash-only due to persistent power cuts (The Telegraph/UNN Jul 11)

Russian Rosstat: consumer gasoline prices rose 6.88% in June 2026 — nearly 10× the May rate; diesel +7.1%; annual gasoline inflation 19.9%; shortages affecting 78 of 83 Russian federal subjects (ISW Jul 11)
Russian federal government allocates ₽4.3B (~$55.9M) emergency subsidies to Crimea tourism companies — reactive fiscal expenditure confirming civilian economic collapse (ISW Jul 11)
Syzran oil refinery (Samara Oblast, 8.5–9M ton/year, supplies Russian Air Force and military in central/southern Russia) struck overnight Jul 11–12 (Ukrainian General Staff/EA WorldView Jul 12)

105 Russian vessels struck in Sea of Azov Jul 6–13; total vessel count fell from 132 to 43; Kerch Strait passage requests suspended; Don-Azov Canal halted — maritime blockade of Crimea effectively complete (ISW Jul 13, SAR imagery)

Kerch Bridge closed 11+ hours overnight Jul 12–13 after drone strikes on Kerch energy point and air defenses — all Crimea supply vectors simultaneously degraded: land corridors, maritime routes, bridge (Euromaidan Press Jul 13)
State fuel procurement system collapsing: ten canceled state fuel tenders across Samara, Tambov, Stavropol; ambulance and utility services running on reserves (Izvestiya via Kyiv Post Jul 13)
Deputy PM Novak publicly acknowledges national fuel crisis ‘due to [Ukrainian] strikes’ — first explicit ministerial attribution (Kyiv Post Jul 13)

El País Feodosia ground-truth Jul 13: fuel reserved exclusively for essential services, QR-code allocation, constant power cuts, food service closed, zero tourism — 70% hotel bookings cancelled (Russian Tourism Industry Union); ‘most expensive retail gasoline on earth’ (Kyiv Post Jul 13)

Pump prices in Yalta and Yevpatoria reach 330–450 rubles/liter ($3.50–5.85/liter, ~$22/gallon) — 3–5× mainland Russian average; grey market 400 rubles/liter doorstep delivery via Telegram (Kyiv Post Jul 13)

Salavat Gazpromneftekhim complex (Bashkortostan, 10M ton/year) struck overnight Jul 13–14 — last major Russian gasoline producer not previously hit in 2026; AVT-6 primary crude distillation unit damaged (ISW/Kyiv Independent Jul 14)

Chubarov (Crimean Tatar Mejlis Chairperson): small and medium enterprises halted operations; livestock farms manually performing previously mechanized processes; water supply affected in northern Crimea (ISW Jul 14)
Razvozhayev announces rotating 2-hours-on/6-hours-off blackouts in Sevastopol following overnight strikes on energy infrastructure (Reuters/Devdiscourse Jul 14)

ATESH: Russian authorities removing 10–40 MVA transformers from idle Northern Crimean Canal pumping stations to replace destroyed substations — spare stocks exhausted; no normal restoration timeline [unverified — partisan source] (Euromaidan Press Jul 14)
VTB reduces ATM withdrawal limits for Crimea customers — banking infrastructure stress in occupied territory (Global Banking & Finance Review Jul 17)
Russian oil refining falls to lowest level since 2005; gasoline production running at ~65% of average seasonal consumption following cumulative strikes (Kyiv Post/Mezha.net Jul 14)

116 vessels struck in Sea of Azov over 9 days as of Jul 14; Ukrainian Navy sinks Russian FSB patrol vessel Izumrud near Novorossiysk using Sargan-3000 unmanned surface vehicle (Kyiv Independent/Euromaidan Press Jul 14)

Kerch railway station warehouses and rolling stock struck Jul 16–17, fires confirmed by NASA satellite data; energy infrastructure in occupied Berdiansk struck; 6–7 shadow-fleet vessels struck in Black Sea (Atesh/USF Jul 17)
SBU strikes shadow-fleet tankers Louise 1 (transported ~3M tonnes Russian crude in 2026) and Banda Jul 16; USF reports 147 vessels struck Jul 6–16 (SBU/USF Jul 17)
